old_claim.note: Writing is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: The surviving map is a cuneiform document whose named places, distance annotations, reverse-side regional descriptions, and colophon are integral to the artifact's mapped world.

source_shape.source_locator: Object 1882,0714.509, Description and Curator's comments: cuneiform labels identify Babylon, the Euphrates, and surrounding regions; notes state distances to eight outer regions; the reverse contains eight descriptive paragraphs; and the colophon says the tablet copied an earlier document.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: Cuneiform labels and explanatory text are constitutive components of the surviving mapped artifact.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The scoped node is not an abstract unlabeled sketch: its geographical identities, outer-region structure, distances, and transmission history are conveyed by writing, so writing is required for this documented artifact.

ontology_before: Writing was treated only as a loose historical predecessor to a broad concept of cartography.
ontology_after: Writing is a required component of the specifically scoped cuneiform world-map tablet.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed and locked by graph invariant 2026-07-16-babylonian-world-map-boundary: writing is the sole direct prerequisite and is required.
would_reject_if: The node is broadened to include non-textual maps rather than the specifically identified cuneiform tablet.
short_reason: The artifact's mapped content depends directly on its cuneiform labels and explanatory text.
